title: Risk and Return Fundamentals
description: Understand the relationship between risk and expected return

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Risk and return are foundational to all financial decisions. CMAs must understand how to quantify risk, how investors demand compensation for bearing it, and how to balance risk and return in capital budgeting, valuation, and performance evaluation.

sections.title: Types of Investment Risk
sections.content:
**Systematic Risk (Market Risk):**
• Affects all securities in market
• Cannot be diversified away
• Examples: Interest rates, inflation, recessions
• Measured by beta

**Unsystematic Risk (Firm-Specific):**
• Unique to individual company
• CAN be diversified away
• Examples: Management changes, lawsuits, product recalls

**Total Risk = Systematic + Unsystematic**

**Key insight:** Investors shouldn't expect extra return for unsystematic risk because it's diversifiable!

sections.title: Measuring Return
sections.content:
**Holding Period Return:**
HPR = (Ending Price - Beginning Price + Dividends) / Beginning Price

**Example:**
Bought at $100, sold at $112, received $4 dividend
HPR = ($112 - $100 + $4) / $100 = **16%**

**Expected Return:**
E(R) = Σ [Probability × Return]

**Example:**
40% chance of 20% return, 60% chance of 8% return
E(R) = (0.40 × 20%) + (0.60 × 8%) = **12.8%**

sections.title: Measuring Risk: Standard Deviation
sections.content:
**Standard Deviation (σ):**
Measures dispersion of returns around the mean

**Formula:**
σ = √[Σ Pi × (Ri - E(R))²]

**Interpretation:**
• Higher σ = More volatile = More risky
• About 68% of returns fall within ±1σ
• About 95% fall within ±2σ

**Example:**
If E(R) = 12% and σ = 8%
Expect returns between 4% and 20% about 68% of the time

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Risk Types
sections.content:
**Systematic vs. Unsystematic:**

**"SYSTEM affects EVERYONE"**
• Systematic = Market-wide = Can't diversify away
• Beta is the measure

**"UNSYS-tem is UN-avoidable without diversification"**
• Unsystematic = Firm-specific = CAN diversify away
• 15-20 stocks eliminates most unsystematic risk

sections.title: Coefficient of Variation
sections.content:
**Formula:**
CV = Standard Deviation / Expected Return

**Why use CV?**
• Standardizes risk per unit of return
• Enables comparison of different-return investments

**Example:**
Investment A: E(R) = 20%, σ = 10%, CV = 0.50
Investment B: E(R) = 10%, σ = 6%, CV = 0.60

**Investment A has lower risk per unit of return** (despite higher absolute risk).

**Lower CV = Better risk-adjusted investment**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Required vs. Expected Return
sections.content:
**Required Return:**
Minimum return investors demand given the risk
(Used for discounting, valuation)

**Expected Return:**
What you actually anticipate earning
(Based on forecasts, probability analysis)

**Investment decision:**
• If Expected > Required → Good investment
• If Expected < Required → Bad investment

**In equilibrium, Expected = Required**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Systematic risk affects all securities; unsystematic is firm-specific
Only systematic risk is compensated (via required return)
Standard deviation measures total risk
Coefficient of variation = σ/E(R) - risk per unit of return
Higher risk requires higher expected return
Diversification eliminates unsystematic risk
15-20 stocks achieves most diversification benefit

title: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
description: Calculate required return using the CAPM framework

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: CAPM is THE model for determining cost of equity. CMAs use it to calculate WACC for capital budgeting, evaluate investment proposals, and assess whether returns compensate adequately for risk. This formula appears constantly on the CMA exam!

sections.title: The CAPM Formula
sections.content:
**Required Return = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)**

**Where:**
• Rf = Risk-free rate (typically T-bill rate)
• β = Beta (systematic risk measure)
• Rm = Expected market return
• (Rm - Rf) = Market risk premium

**Example:**
Rf = 3%, β = 1.2, Rm = 10%
Required Return = 3% + 1.2(10% - 3%)
= 3% + 1.2(7%)
= 3% + 8.4% = **11.4%**

sections.title: Understanding Beta
sections.content:
**Beta measures systematic risk relative to market:**

**β = 1.0:** Same volatility as market
**β > 1.0:** More volatile than market (aggressive)
**β < 1.0:** Less volatile than market (defensive)
**β = 0:** No correlation with market (risk-free)
**β < 0:** Moves opposite to market (rare)

**Examples:**
• Technology: β ≈ 1.3 (high)
• Utilities: β ≈ 0.5 (low)
• S&P 500 index: β = 1.0 (by definition)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CAPM
sections.content:
**"RRR = RF + β(MRP)"**

**R**equired **R**eturn = **R**isk-**F**ree + **B**eta × **M**arket **R**isk **P**remium

**Think of it as:**
Base compensation (risk-free rate)
+ Risk compensation (beta × premium)
= Total required return

**Higher beta → Higher required return!**

sections.title: Security Market Line (SML)
sections.content:
**The SML graphs CAPM:**
• X-axis: Beta (β)
• Y-axis: Required return
• Y-intercept: Risk-free rate
• Slope: Market risk premium

**Interpretation:**
• Stocks ON the SML are fairly priced
• Stocks ABOVE the SML are undervalued (buy)
• Stocks BELOW the SML are overvalued (sell)

**All properly priced securities should lie on the SML!**

sections.title: Limitations of CAPM
sections.content:
**Assumptions that may not hold:**

• Single-period model
• All investors have same expectations
• Perfect markets (no taxes, transaction costs)
• Investors can borrow at risk-free rate
• Beta is stable over time

**Practical issues:**
• Which risk-free rate? (T-bill, 10-year?)
• Historical vs. forward-looking beta?
• Market risk premium estimate varies widely

**Despite limitations, CAPM remains widely used!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Levered vs. Unlevered Beta
sections.content:
**βL = βU × [1 + (1-t)(D/E)]**

**βL (Levered):** Includes financial risk from debt
**βU (Unlevered):** Pure business risk only

**Why it matters:**
When comparing firms with different capital structures,
unlever betas first to isolate business risk.

**More debt → Higher levered beta → Higher cost of equity**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CAPM: Required Return = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)
Beta measures systematic (market) risk only
Beta = 1 means same risk as market
SML plots required return vs. beta
Stocks should lie on SML if properly priced
CAPM gives cost of equity for WACC calculation
Levered beta includes financial risk from debt

title: Cost of Capital
description: Calculate weighted average cost of capital (WACC)

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: WACC is the hurdle rate for investment decisions. A project must earn at least the WACC to create value. CMAs calculate WACC for capital budgeting, valuation, and performance metrics like EVA. Getting WACC wrong leads to bad investment decisions!

sections.title: The WACC Formula
sections.content:
**WACC = (E/V × Re) + (D/V × Rd × (1-t)) + (P/V × Rp)**

**Where:**
• E/V = Weight of equity
• D/V = Weight of debt
• P/V = Weight of preferred stock
• Re = Cost of equity
• Rd = Cost of debt
• Rp = Cost of preferred
• t = Tax rate
• V = E + D + P (total value)

**Debt gets tax benefit; equity and preferred do not!**

sections.title: Cost of Debt
sections.content:
**Rd = Interest rate on new debt × (1 - Tax Rate)**

**Finding the rate:**
• Yield to maturity on existing bonds
• Interest rate on new borrowing
• Add credit spread to benchmark

**Example:**
New bank loan rate: 6%
Tax rate: 25%
After-tax cost of debt: 6% × (1 - 0.25) = **4.5%**

**Use MARGINAL (new) debt cost, not historical!**

sections.title: Dividend Growth Model Example
sections.content:
**Gordon Model:**
Re = D₁/P₀ + g

**Where:**
• D₁ = Next year's dividend (D₀ × (1+g))
• P₀ = Current stock price
• g = Sustainable dividend growth rate

**Example:**
Current dividend: $2.00
Expected growth: 5%
Stock price: $50

D₁ = $2.00 × 1.05 = $2.10
Re = ($2.10 / $50) + 5% = 4.2% + 5% = **9.2%**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: WACC
sections.content:
**"Weighted costs, Adjusted for tax"**

**E**quity: Get Re from CAPM or dividend model
**D**ebt: After-tax! Rd × (1-t)
**P**referred: Dp / Pp (no tax benefit)

**Weight by MARKET values, not book values!**

**WACC = Minimum return to satisfy ALL capital providers**

sections.title: Complete WACC Calculation
sections.content:
**Example:**
• Market value of equity: $60 million
• Market value of debt: $40 million
• Total: $100 million
• Cost of equity (CAPM): 12%
• Cost of debt: 6%
• Tax rate: 25%

**Weights:**
E/V = 60/100 = 60%
D/V = 40/100 = 40%

**WACC:**
= (0.60 × 12%) + (0.40 × 6% × 0.75)
= 7.2% + 1.8%
= **9.0%**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Market vs. Book Values
sections.content:
**Always use MARKET values for weights!**

**Why?**
• WACC is forward-looking
• Investors care about market values
• Book values are historical

**Exception:**
• If market values unavailable, exam may allow book
• Private companies may use book with adjustment

**The question will usually state which to use!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: WACC = Weighted average of debt, equity, and preferred costs
Debt is tax-advantaged: use Rd × (1-t)
Cost of equity: CAPM or Dividend Growth Model
Use MARKET value weights, not book values
WACC is the hurdle rate for investment decisions
Projects earning > WACC create shareholder value
Use marginal (new) costs, not historical

title: Capital Structure Theory
description: Understand optimal capital structure and leverage effects

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How should we finance the company - debt or equity? Capital structure decisions affect cost of capital, financial risk, and shareholder value. CMAs analyze financing alternatives, understand leverage effects, and advise on optimal capital structure.

sections.title: Modigliani-Miller Propositions
sections.content:
**MM Without Taxes (Perfect Markets):**
• Proposition I: Capital structure is irrelevant to firm value
• Proposition II: Cost of equity increases with leverage

**MM With Taxes:**
• Proposition I: Firm value increases with debt (tax shield)
• Proposition II: Cost of equity still rises, but debt is cheaper

**Implication with taxes:**
Value of levered firm = Value of unlevered firm + PV(Tax Shield)

**More debt → More value (in MM world)**

sections.title: Why MM Breaks Down in Reality
sections.content:
**Real-world frictions:**

**Bankruptcy costs:**
• Direct (legal, administrative)
• Indirect (lost customers, damaged reputation)

**Agency costs:**
• Debtholders restrict management
• Monitoring and covenant compliance

**Information asymmetry:**
• Signals about firm quality
• Market reactions to financing choices

**These create an OPTIMAL capital structure!**

sections.title: Trade-Off Theory
sections.content:
**Balance tax benefits vs. distress costs:**

Value = Unlevered value + Tax shield - Distress costs

**At optimal structure:**
• Marginal tax benefit = Marginal distress cost
• Adding more debt destroys value
• Each firm has different optimum

**Optimal debt increases when:**
• Higher tax rate
• More tangible assets (collateral)
• Stable cash flows
• Lower distress probability

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capital Structure Theories
sections.content:
**"TPM" - Three Main Theories:**

**T**rade-Off: Balance tax benefits vs. distress costs
**P**ecking Order: Internal funds → Debt → Equity
**M**arket Timing: Issue when prices are favorable

**Trade-off says there's an optimum.
Pecking order says follow a hierarchy.
Market timing says opportunistically issue securities.**

sections.title: Pecking Order Theory
rows: No transaction costs, no asymmetric info

sections.title: Financial Leverage Effects
sections.content:
**Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL):**
DFL = EBIT / (EBIT - Interest)

**Or:**
% Change in EPS / % Change in EBIT

**What it means:**
If DFL = 2.0, a 10% increase in EBIT causes a 20% increase in EPS.

**Double-edged sword:**
• Amplifies gains when EBIT rises
• Amplifies losses when EBIT falls
• Higher DFL = Higher financial risk

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Leverage Types
sections.content:
**Operating Leverage (DOL):**
• Fixed operating costs
• Magnifies EBIT relative to sales

**Financial Leverage (DFL):**
• Fixed financing costs (interest)
• Magnifies EPS relative to EBIT

**Combined Leverage (DCL):**
DCL = DOL × DFL

**Magnifies EPS relative to sales!
High combined leverage = Very risky!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: MM: Capital structure irrelevant in perfect markets
With taxes: Debt increases value via tax shield
Trade-off theory: Optimal structure balances tax benefits vs. distress costs
Pecking order: Internal funds → Debt → Equity
DFL measures earnings amplification from leverage
Combined leverage = DOL × DFL
Real-world frictions create optimal capital structure

title: Working Capital Management
description: Optimize current assets and liabilities for liquidity and profitability

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Working capital is the lifeblood of daily operations. Too little means cash crises; too much means wasted resources. CMAs optimize working capital to balance liquidity and profitability - freeing cash while keeping operations running smoothly.

sections.title: Working Capital Basics
sections.content:
**Net Working Capital (NWC):**
NWC = Current Assets - Current Liabilities

**Operating Working Capital:**
OWC = Current Assets - Cash - Current Liabilities + Notes Payable

**Components:**
• Cash and marketable securities
• Accounts receivable
• Inventory
• Accounts payable
• Accrued expenses

**Goal:** Minimize investment while maintaining operations

sections.title: Cash Conversion Cycle Review
sections.content:
**CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO**

**Days Inventory Outstanding:** How long to sell
**Days Sales Outstanding:** How long to collect
**Days Payable Outstanding:** How long to pay suppliers

**Example:**
DIO: 45 days, DSO: 30 days, DPO: 25 days
CCC = 45 + 30 - 25 = **50 days**

**Lower CCC = Less cash tied up = Better!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Working Capital Trade-Offs
sections.content:
**"LIPRIP"**

**L**iquidity vs. **P**rofitability
Holding more CA → More liquid but less profitable (tied up cash)

**R**isk vs. **R**eturn
More ST debt → Higher return but higher risk (refinancing)

**I**nventory **P**olicy
Lower inventory → Less carrying cost but more stockout risk

**It's all about balance!**

sections.title: Cash Management
sections.content:
**Objectives:**
• Maintain sufficient liquidity
• Minimize idle cash balance
• Invest excess cash efficiently
• Manage collections and disbursements

**Strategies:**
• Accelerate collections (lockboxes, EFT)
• Delay disbursements (centralized, controlled)
• Concentrate cash (single account)
• Invest excess in money market instruments

**Zero-balance accounts:** Maintain minimal balance, fund as needed

sections.title: Credit Policy Trade-Offs
sections.content:
**Loosening credit policy:**

**Benefits:**
• Increased sales
• Competitive advantage
• Customer loyalty

**Costs:**
• Higher bad debts
• Higher carrying cost (AR investment)
• Delayed cash receipts

**Optimal policy:** Marginal benefit = Marginal cost

**Variables to manage:**
• Credit standards (who qualifies)
• Credit terms (payment period, discounts)
• Collection policy (aggressiveness)

sections.title: Evaluating Trade Credit
sections.content:
**Terms: 2/10, net 30**
• 2% discount if paid within 10 days
• Full amount due in 30 days

**Annual cost of not taking discount:**
= (Discount % / (100 - Discount %)) × (365 / (Full period - Discount period))

**Example:**
= (2 / 98) × (365 / 20)
= 0.0204 × 18.25
= **37.2% per year!**

**Almost always take the discount if you have cash!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Short-Term Financing Sources
sections.content:
**Know the options and costs:**

**Trade credit:** Free if discounts taken, expensive if not
**Bank lines:** Commitment fee + interest
**Commercial paper:** Lowest cost, but only for creditworthy
**Factoring:** Selling receivables - expensive but immediate
**Secured loans:** Pledging inventory/receivables

**Compare EFFECTIVE annual rates to choose!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NWC = Current Assets - Current Liabilities
CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO (lower is better)
Aggressive policy: Low CA, high CL (risky but profitable)
Conservative policy: High CA, low CL (safe but costly)
Trade credit cost can be 30%+ if discount not taken
Balance liquidity vs. profitability
Working capital optimization frees cash for investment

title: Raising Capital - Debt
description: Understand sources and characteristics of debt financing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Debt is typically cheaper than equity due to tax benefits and lower risk to investors. CMAs evaluate debt alternatives, negotiate terms, monitor covenants, and understand when debt is appropriate. Choosing the wrong debt structure can constrain the company or trigger default.

sections.title: Bank Loans
sections.content:
**Types:**

**Revolving credit:** Draw and repay as needed (like credit card)
**Term loan:** Fixed principal and interest payments
**Bridge loan:** Short-term until permanent financing

**Key terms:**
• Interest rate (fixed or variable)
• Commitment fee (on unused portion)
• Compensating balance requirements
• Covenants (restrictions)
• Collateral/security

**Calculate EFFECTIVE rate including all costs!**

sections.title: Bond Characteristics
sections.content:
**Key features:**

**Par/Face value:** Amount repaid at maturity ($1,000 typical)
**Coupon rate:** Annual interest as % of par
**Maturity:** Length until principal repaid
**Call provision:** Issuer can repay early
**Sinking fund:** Required periodic repayments

**Bond types:**
• Secured (backed by assets) vs. Unsecured (debentures)
• Senior vs. Subordinated
• Fixed vs. Floating rate
• Zero-coupon (no periodic interest)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Bond Prices
sections.content:
**"PRIDE"** determines bond price:

**P**ar value (face amount)
**R**equired return (market rate)
**I**nterest rate (coupon)
**D**ate of maturity
**E**conomic conditions

**When market rate > coupon rate: Bond sells at DISCOUNT**
**When market rate < coupon rate: Bond sells at PREMIUM**
**When market rate = coupon rate: Bond sells at PAR**

sections.title: Bond Valuation
sections.content:
**Price = PV of coupons + PV of face value**

P = C × [1 - (1+r)^-n] / r + FV/(1+r)^n

**Example:**
• Face value: $1,000
• Coupon: 6% annual ($60/year)
• Maturity: 5 years
• Market rate: 8%

P = $60 × [1 - (1.08)^-5] / 0.08 + $1,000/(1.08)^5
P = $60 × 3.993 + $1,000 × 0.681
P = $239.58 + $680.58 = **$920.16**

**(Discount because market rate > coupon)**

sections.title: Debt Covenants
sections.content:
**Affirmative covenants (must do):**
• Maintain insurance
• Provide financial statements
• Pay taxes on time
• Maintain certain ratios

**Negative covenants (can't do):**
• Limit additional borrowing
• Restrict dividends
• Limit asset sales
• Prohibit mergers without consent

**Violation → Technical default → Lender can accelerate payment!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Effective Interest Rate
sections.content:
**Include ALL costs in effective rate:**

**Compensating balance:**
Effective rate = Interest / (Loan - Required balance)

**Discount basis:**
Effective rate = Interest / (Face - Interest)

**Example:**
$100,000 loan at 8%, 10% compensating balance
Usable funds: $90,000
Interest: $8,000
Effective rate: $8,000 / $90,000 = **8.89%**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Debt sources range from trade credit to long-term bonds
Bank loans: revolving credit, term loans, bridge financing
Bond price: PV of coupon payments + PV of principal
Market rate > coupon → Discount; Market rate < coupon → Premium
Covenants protect lenders but restrict management
Calculate effective rate including all fees and requirements
Debt is tax-advantaged but adds financial risk

title: Raising Capital - Equity
description: Understand equity financing sources and considerations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Equity is permanent capital with no required payments, but it dilutes ownership and is expensive. CMAs evaluate when equity is appropriate, assess dilution effects, and understand shareholder expectations. Equity decisions affect ownership, control, and long-term value.

sections.title: Common Stock Characteristics
sections.content:
**Rights of common shareholders:**

• Voting rights (board election, major decisions)
• Residual claim on assets (after creditors and preferred)
• Dividend rights (if declared)
• Preemptive rights (maintain ownership % in new issues)

**Key terms:**
• Par value (legal minimum, often $0.01)
• No fixed dividend obligation
• Subordinate to all other claims in liquidation

**No maturity - permanent capital!**

sections.title: Common Stock Valuation
sections.content:
**Dividend Discount Model (DDM):**
P₀ = D₁ / (r - g)

**Where:**
• D₁ = Expected dividend next period
• r = Required return
• g = Dividend growth rate

**Example:**
D₀ = $2.00, g = 6%, r = 11%
D₁ = $2.00 × 1.06 = $2.12
P₀ = $2.12 / (0.11 - 0.06) = $2.12 / 0.05 = **$42.40**

**Requires r > g for model to work!**

sections.title: Preferred Stock Valuation
sections.content:
**Perpetual preferred (no maturity):**
P = D / r

**Where:**
• D = Annual preferred dividend
• r = Required return on preferred

**Example:**
Preferred dividend: $5 per year
Required return: 8%
P = $5 / 0.08 = **$62.50**

**Simpler than common stock valuation because dividend is fixed!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Equity vs. Debt
sections.content:
**Debt: "SCOPED"**
**S**enior claim, **C**ontractual payments, **O**bligatory, **P**rincipal due, **E**xpense (tax deductible), **D**efault risk

**Equity: "PREVR"**
**P**ermanent, **R**esidual claim, **E**xpensive (no tax benefit), **V**oting rights, **R**isky for investor (last in line)

**Debt is cheaper but riskier for company.
Equity is safer but more expensive!**

sections.title: Going Public (IPO)
sections.content:
**Initial Public Offering process:**

1. Select investment bank (underwriter)
2. Due diligence and registration (SEC)
3. Roadshow (marketing to investors)
4. Price setting
5. Distribution

**Costs:**
• Underwriting spread (6-7% typical)
• Legal and accounting fees
• Ongoing compliance costs
• Information disclosure requirements

**Benefits:** Access capital, liquidity, prestige
**Drawbacks:** Cost, scrutiny, pressure for short-term results

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Stock Issuance Costs
sections.content:
**Flotation costs reduce net proceeds:**

Effective cost of equity = D₁ / (P₀ × (1-F)) + g

**Where F = flotation cost %**

**Example:**
P₀ = $40, D₁ = $2, g = 6%, F = 5%
Net proceeds = $40 × 0.95 = $38
Cost of equity = $2/$38 + 6% = 5.26% + 6% = **11.26%**

**vs. 11% without flotation costs!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Common stock: Voting rights, residual claim, no fixed dividend
Common stock value: P₀ = D₁/(r-g) (Gordon model)
Preferred stock: Fixed dividend, no voting, senior to common
Preferred value: P = D/r (perpetuity formula)
Equity is permanent capital - no maturity
IPO costs ~7% in underwriting plus compliance
Flotation costs increase effective cost of equity

title: Dividend Policy
description: Understand dividend decisions and their impact on value

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Do dividends matter? This question has sparked decades of debate. CMAs advise on dividend policy, analyze the trade-offs between paying dividends vs. reinvesting, and understand how dividend decisions signal information to the market.

sections.title: Dividend Irrelevance Theory
sections.content:
**Miller & Modigliani (MM):**
In perfect markets, dividend policy doesn't affect firm value.

**Logic:**
• Shareholders can create "homemade dividends" by selling shares
• If company needs cash for investment, it can issue new shares
• Total returns = dividends + capital gains
• Mix doesn't matter; total is what counts

**Assumptions (often violated):**
• No taxes
• No transaction costs
• No information asymmetry

sections.title: Dividend Payment Process
sections.content:
**Key dates:**

**Declaration date:** Board announces dividend
**Ex-dividend date:** Stock trades without dividend (2 days before record)
**Record date:** Shareholders on record receive dividend
**Payment date:** Dividend actually paid

**Stock price typically drops by dividend amount on ex-date!**

**Example:**
$50 stock, $1 dividend
Ex-date: Stock opens at ~$49

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dividend Dates
sections.content:
**"DERP" (in order):**

**D**eclaration - Board decides
**E**x-dividend - Buy before to get dividend!
**R**ecord - Must own to qualify
**P**ayment - Money in hand

**Key:** Buy BEFORE ex-date to receive the dividend.
Ex-date is 2 business days before record date.

sections.title: Stock Dividends vs. Stock Splits
sections.content:
**Stock dividend:**
• Issue additional shares to existing shareholders
• Example: 10% stock dividend = 10 new shares per 100 owned
• Transfer from retained earnings to capital stock

**Stock split:**
• Increase shares by proportional factor
• Example: 2-for-1 split doubles shares, halves price
• No accounting entry (just more shares at lower par)

**Both:** No cash changes hands, proportional ownership unchanged, total value same

sections.title: Common Payout Policies
sections.content:
**Stable/Constant dividend:**
• Same $ per share each period
• Slowly increase over time
• Market expects consistency

**Constant payout ratio:**
• Fixed % of earnings
• Dividend fluctuates with earnings
• Less common (investors dislike volatility)

**Residual dividend policy:**
• Pay dividends from leftover after investments
• Mathematically optimal but volatile

**Most companies target stable, growing dividends!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Share Repurchases
sections.content:
**Repurchases are an alternative to dividends:**

**Advantages:**
• Flexibility (not "expected" like dividends)
• Tax advantage (capital gains vs. ordinary income)
• Signals undervaluation
• Increases EPS (fewer shares)

**Effect on EPS:**
EPS = Earnings / Fewer shares = Higher EPS

**But: Repurchases don't create value by themselves - they're a cash distribution method!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: MM: Dividend policy irrelevant in perfect markets
Real world: Taxes, signals, and clienteles matter
Buy before ex-date to receive dividend
Stock dividends/splits don't change total value
Most firms prefer stable, growing dividend policy
Residual policy: Pay from leftover after investments
Share repurchases are an alternative distribution method

title: Leasing Decisions
description: Analyze lease vs. buy decisions and lease accounting implications

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Leasing is a major source of financing that affects balance sheets, ROA, and capital budgeting decisions. CMAs evaluate lease vs. buy alternatives, understand the accounting treatment, and analyze the financial statement impact under ASC 842.

sections.title: Types of Leases
rows: ROU asset and liability on BS under ASC 842
May recognize gain if at fair value

sections.title: Finance Lease Criteria (ASC 842)
sections.content:
**Any ONE of these makes it a finance lease:**

1. **Ownership transfer** at end of lease
2. **Bargain purchase option** (likely to be exercised)
3. **Lease term** ≥ 75% of economic life
4. **PV of payments** ≥ 90% of fair value
5. **Specialized asset** with no alternative use to lessor

**Otherwise: Operating lease**

**But under ASC 842, both types go on the balance sheet!**

sections.title: Lease vs. Buy Analysis
sections.content:
**Net Advantage to Leasing (NAL):**
NAL = PV of owning - PV of leasing

**If NAL > 0:** Owning is cheaper
**If NAL < 0:** Leasing is cheaper

**Owning costs:**
• Purchase price
• - Tax depreciation benefit
• - After-tax maintenance
• + After-tax salvage value

**Leasing costs:**
• After-tax lease payments

**Discount at after-tax cost of debt (secured borrowing rate)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Lease Criteria
sections.content:
**"BOTLS"** - Finance lease if any one is met:

**B**argain purchase option
**O**wnership transfers
**T**erm ≥ 75% of asset life
**L**arge PV (≥ 90% of fair value)
**S**pecialized asset

**If none met → Operating lease
(But still on balance sheet under ASC 842!)**

sections.title: Balance Sheet Impact (ASC 842)
sections.content:
**For BOTH finance and operating leases:**

**Assets:**
Right-of-use (ROU) asset = PV of lease payments

**Liabilities:**
Lease liability = PV of lease payments

**Key change from old rules:**
Operating leases were off-balance sheet (rent expense only)
Now on-balance sheet (grosses up both assets and liabilities)

**Impact on ratios:**
• Debt ratios increase
• Asset turnover decreases
• Current ratio may decrease

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Discount Rate for Lease
sections.content:
**Lessee uses:**

1. **Rate implicit in lease** (if readily determinable)
2. **Incremental borrowing rate** (if implicit rate unknown)

**Incremental borrowing rate:**
Rate lessee would pay for similar secured loan

**For lease vs. buy analysis:**
Use after-tax cost of debt!
(Because lease is a financing decision, not an investment)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Finance lease: meets any of BOTLS criteria
Under ASC 842, ALL leases are on balance sheet
ROU asset = Lease liability = PV of payments
Finance lease: front-loaded expense (interest + amortization)
Operating lease: straight-line expense
Lease vs. buy: compare PV at after-tax debt cost
Impact: Higher liabilities, lower turnover ratios

title: Mergers and Acquisitions
description: Understand M&A valuation, financing, and strategic considerations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: M&A represents the largest transactions a company may undertake. CMAs evaluate acquisition targets, determine fair values, assess synergies, analyze financing options, and perform post-acquisition integration. These decisions can create or destroy billions in value.

sections.title: Valuation Methods
sections.content:
**1. Comparable Companies Analysis:**
Apply multiples from similar companies (P/E, EV/EBITDA)

**2. Precedent Transactions:**
Prices paid in similar deals (includes control premium)

**3. DCF Valuation:**
PV of expected free cash flows + Terminal value

**Key formula:**
Value = FCF₁/(1+r) + FCF₂/(1+r)² +...+ TV/(1+r)ⁿ

**Where TV = FCFn+1 / (r - g) (Gordon growth)**

sections.title: Synergy Analysis
sections.content:
**Types of synergies:**

**Cost synergies (easier to achieve):**
• Headcount reduction
• Facility consolidation
• Purchasing power
• Technology rationalization

**Revenue synergies (harder to achieve):**
• Cross-selling
• Geographic expansion
• Product line expansion

**Key principle:**
Value of A + B + Synergies - Premium = Value created

**"Synergies" often overestimated!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: M&A Value Creation
sections.content:
**"CRISP"** - Sources of M&A value:

**C**ost reductions (synergies)
**R**evenue enhancements
**I**mproved management
**S**trategic positioning
**P**urchase price (must be less than value created!)

**Value created = Synergy value - Premium paid**
Most value often goes to selling shareholders!

sections.title: Deal Structures
sections.content:
**Payment methods:**

**Cash:** Simpler, but depletes resources
**Stock:** Shares dilution, but preserves cash
**Combination:** Balance of both

**Structure types:**

**Asset purchase:** Buy specific assets/liabilities
• Step-up basis for buyer
• Seller may have double taxation

**Stock purchase:** Buy shares of target
• Carryover basis
• Simpler but inherit all liabilities

sections.title: Acquisition Premium
sections.content:
**Premium = (Offer Price - Market Price) / Market Price**

**Example:**
Target trading at $40
Offer price: $52
Premium: ($52 - $40) / $40 = **30%**

**Why pay a premium?**
• Control value (can make changes)
• Synergy value
• Competition from other bidders

**Typical premiums: 20-40%**

**The acquirer must capture synergies > premium to create value!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Goodwill
sections.content:
**Purchase price exceeds fair value of net assets:**

**Goodwill = Purchase Price - FV of Net Identifiable Assets**

**Example:**
Purchase price: $100 million
FV of assets: $120 million
FV of liabilities: $40 million
Net identifiable assets: $80 million
Goodwill: $100M - $80M = **$20 million**

**Under GAAP, goodwill is NOT amortized but tested annually for impairment!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Horizontal M&A = same industry; Vertical = supply chain
Valuation: Comparables, Precedent transactions, DCF
Synergies: Cost (easier) and Revenue (harder)
Value created = Synergies - Premium paid
Payment: Cash depletes resources; Stock dilutes ownership
Asset purchase: step-up basis; Stock purchase: carryover
Goodwill = Price - FV of net identifiable assets (test for impairment)

title: International Finance
description: Manage foreign exchange risk and understand international financial concepts

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Global operations expose companies to foreign exchange risk that can wipe out operating profits overnight. CMAs must understand how exchange rates work, how to hedge currency exposure, and the parity conditions that drive international financial markets. This is an increasingly tested area on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange Rate Quotations
sections.content:
**Direct Quote (American Terms):**
Domestic currency per unit of foreign currency
Example: $1.25/€1 (it costs $1.25 to buy one euro)

**Indirect Quote (European Terms):**
Foreign currency per unit of domestic currency
Example: €0.80/$1 (one dollar buys 0.80 euros)

**Key relationship:**
Direct quote = 1 / Indirect quote
$1.25/€ = 1 / (€0.80/$)

**Cross Rates:**
Derived from two quotes involving a common currency
If $1.25/€ and $1.50/£, then:
€ per £ = $1.50 / $1.25 = €1.20/£

**Currency Appreciation/Depreciation:**
• If rate goes from $1.25/€ to $1.30/€ → Euro appreciated (costs more dollars)
• If rate goes from $1.25/€ to $1.20/€ → Euro depreciated (costs fewer dollars)

**Tip:** In a direct quote, if the number goes UP, the foreign currency got STRONGER.

sections.title: Spot vs. Forward Rates
sections.content:
**Spot Rate:**
• Exchange rate for IMMEDIATE delivery (settlement in 2 business days)
• Reflects current supply and demand

**Forward Rate:**
• Exchange rate agreed TODAY for exchange at a FUTURE date
• Used to lock in exchange rates and hedge risk
• Common maturities: 30, 60, 90, 180 days, 1 year

**Forward Premium/Discount:**
Forward premium (%) = [(Forward - Spot) / Spot] × (360/days) × 100

**Example:**
Spot: $1.2500/€
90-day forward: $1.2600/€
Premium = [($1.2600 - $1.2500) / $1.2500] × (360/90) × 100
= (0.008) × 4 × 100 = **3.2% annualized premium**

**The euro is at a forward PREMIUM** (more expensive in the future).

**If forward < spot → Foreign currency is at a forward DISCOUNT**

sections.title: Interest Rate Parity (IRP)
sections.content:
**The forward rate reflects the interest rate differential between two countries.**

**Formula:**
Forward / Spot = (1 + Rdomestic) / (1 + Rforeign)

**Where R = interest rate for the period**

**Example:**
Spot: $1.2500/€
US 1-year rate: 5%
Eurozone 1-year rate: 3%

Forward = $1.2500 × (1.05/1.03) = $1.2500 × 1.0194 = **$1.2743/€**

**Interpretation:**
• Higher US interest rates → Dollar at forward discount
• The euro trades at a forward premium of ~1.94%
• This premium offsets the interest rate advantage

**Key Insight:**
IRP ensures you earn the same return whether you:
1. Invest domestically, OR
2. Convert to foreign currency, invest abroad, and hedge with a forward

**If IRP doesn't hold → Covered interest arbitrage opportunity exists!**

sections.title: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
sections.content:
**Exchange rates adjust to equalize purchasing power across countries.**

**Absolute PPP:**
A basket of goods should cost the same in all countries when converted to a common currency.
(Rarely holds due to trade barriers, transportation, non-traded goods)

**Relative PPP (More useful):**
Exchange rate changes reflect INFLATION rate differentials.

**Formula:**
(Expected Spot₁ - Spot₀) / Spot₀ ≈ Inflation_domestic - Inflation_foreign

**Example:**
Current spot: $1.2500/€
US expected inflation: 4%
Eurozone expected inflation: 2%

Expected change: 4% - 2% = 2%
Expected future spot: $1.2500 × 1.02 = **$1.2750/€**

**Interpretation:**
• Higher US inflation → Dollar weakens (depreciates)
• The currency of the higher-inflation country depreciates

**PPP explains long-run exchange rate trends but NOT short-run movements.**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: IRP vs. PPP
sections.content:
**"Interest drives FORWARDS, Prices drive SPOTS"**

**Interest Rate Parity (IRP):**
• Links interest rates to FORWARD rates
• Higher interest → Forward discount for that currency
• Holds well in practice (arbitrage enforces it)

**Purchasing Power Parity (PPP):**
• Links inflation to SPOT rate changes
• Higher inflation → Currency depreciates
• Works in long-run, not short-run

**Both say the same thing differently:**
The country with higher rates/inflation sees its currency weaken to offset the advantage.

sections.title: Transaction vs. Translation Exposure
sections.content:
**Transaction Exposure:**
• Risk from outstanding obligations in foreign currency
• Affects CASH FLOW
• Example: US company owes €1M to German supplier in 90 days
• If euro strengthens, the dollar cost INCREASES

**Hedging transaction exposure:**
1. **Forward contract** — Lock in the exchange rate today
2. **Money market hedge** — Borrow/invest to create offsetting position
3. **Currency options** — Buy the right (not obligation) to exchange
4. **Natural hedge** — Match foreign currency revenues with expenses

**Translation Exposure (Accounting Exposure):**
• Risk from converting foreign subsidiary financial statements to parent currency
• Affects REPORTED EARNINGS, not cash flow
• Under GAAP: Current rate method or temporal method

**Current Rate Method (most common):**
• Assets/Liabilities → Current exchange rate
• Equity → Historical rate
• Revenue/Expenses → Average rate
• Translation adjustment → Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)

**Economic Exposure:**
• Long-term impact of exchange rate changes on firm value
• Affects competitive position and future cash flows
• Hardest to measure and hedge

sections.title: Euromarkets
sections.content:
**Eurocurrency Market:**
• Deposits of a currency held OUTSIDE its home country
• Eurodollars = US dollars deposited outside the US (e.g., in London)
• Euroyen = Yen deposited outside Japan
• NOT related to the euro currency or Europe specifically!

**Why Euromarkets exist:**
• Less regulation than domestic markets
• Competitive interest rates (higher deposit, lower lending)
• Tax advantages in some jurisdictions
• 24-hour global access

**Eurobonds:**
• Bonds issued in a currency different from the country of issuance
• Example: A US company issues bonds denominated in yen in London
• Advantages: Avoid domestic registration, access global investors

**Foreign Bonds (contrast):**
• Issued in a foreign country, in THAT country's currency
• Yankee bonds: Foreign issuer, USD, in US market
• Samurai bonds: Foreign issuer, yen, in Japan
• Bulldog bonds: Foreign issuer, GBP, in UK

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Exchange Rate Direction
sections.content:
**The #1 source of errors on international finance questions:**

**Direct vs. Indirect quote confusion:**
• $1.25/€ (direct) — dollar cost of euros
• €0.80/$ (indirect) — how many euros per dollar

**Who benefits from what?**
• US EXPORTER benefits when dollar WEAKENS (foreign buyers get more for their money)
• US IMPORTER benefits when dollar STRENGTHENS (foreign goods are cheaper)

**Hedging direction:**
• If you OWE foreign currency → BUY it forward (lock in your cost)
• If you are OWED foreign currency → SELL it forward (lock in your revenue)

**Forward premium/discount shortcut:**
• Higher interest rate country → Currency at forward DISCOUNT
• Lower interest rate country → Currency at forward PREMIUM
• Remember: The forward rate OFFSETS the interest rate advantage

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Direct quote = domestic per foreign; Indirect = foreign per domestic
Forward rate locks in a future exchange rate — used for hedging
Interest Rate Parity: Forward rates reflect interest rate differentials
Purchasing Power Parity: Exchange rates adjust for inflation differences (long-run)
Transaction exposure affects cash flows; translation exposure affects reported earnings
Hedge transaction exposure with forwards, money market hedges, or options
Eurodollars are USD deposited outside the US — not related to the euro currency
Higher interest rate country's currency trades at a forward discount
